Apple___Apple_scab.description: A common fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It affects leaves, fruit, and twigs, causing olive-green to brown spots. Severe infections lead to defoliation and misshapen, scabby fruit.
precautions: Rake up and destroy fallen leaves in autumn to reduce overwintering fungus.
Prune trees for good air circulation.
Water at the base, avoid wetting foliage.
risk_factors: Wet spring weather (spores released during rain).
Presence of infected leaves or fruit from the previous season.

chemical: Apply fungicides (e.g., captan, mancozeb, myclobutanil, sulfur) starting early spring before bloom and continue on schedule based on weather/label.
Rotation of fungicides is important.
organic: Neem oil can offer some suppression.

Apple___Black_rot.description: A fungal disease (Botryosphaeria obtusa) causing fruit rot, leaf spots ('frogeye leaf spot'), and branch cankers. Fruit rot starts at the blossom end or a wound, turning brown then black and often shriveling into a mummy.
precautions: Prune out dead or cankered branches during dormancy, cutting well below the canker.
Remove and destroy mummified fruit hanging on the tree or on the ground.
Maintain good air circulation through pruning.
Control insects that cause fruit wounds.
risk_factors: Presence of dead wood or cankers on the tree.
Mummified fruit left from previous season.
Warm, humid, and wet weather, especially during bloom and fruit development.

chemical: Fungicides used for apple scab (captan, mancozeb, strobilurins) applied from bloom through harvest often provide control.
Specific timing based on weather is crucial.
organic: Sanitation (removing infected wood/fruit) is the most effective organic control.
Copper sprays (dormant) might offer slight protection.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: A fungal disease (Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae) requiring both apple/crabapple and juniper/cedar hosts. Causes bright orange/yellow spots on apple leaves and fruit.
precautions: Remove nearby susceptible juniper/cedar trees (often impractical).
Avoid planting susceptible apple varieties near junipers.
risk_factors: Presence of susceptible juniper hosts within 1-2 miles.
Wet, moderate spring weather (around 46-75°F or 8-24°C) when spores are released from cedar galls.

chemical: Apply preventative fungicides (e.g., myclobutanil, fenarimol, mancozeb) starting at pink bud stage and continuing through early fruit development, timed based on spore release.
Consult local spray guides.
organic: Sulfur sprays can offer some protection if applied preventatively.

Apple___healthy.description: The apple leaf appears healthy, with good color and no signs of spots, lesions, or stress.
precautions: Monitor regularly for pests and diseases.
Ensure proper pruning, watering, and fertilization.
risk_factors: Weather conditions favorable to common diseases (scab, rust).

Blueberry___healthy.description: The blueberry plant appears healthy, with characteristic green leaves and no signs of disease or significant pest damage.
precautions: Prune annually to remove old canes and improve air circulation.
Mulch to conserve moisture and suppress weeds.
Monitor for common issues like mummy berry, stem blight.
risk_factors: Soil pH outside the optimal range.
Wet weather during bloom (mummy berry risk).

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Fungal disease (Podosphaera clandestina) causing white, powdery patches on leaves, shoots, and sometimes fruit. Can cause leaf distortion, yellowing, and reduced growth.
risk_factors: High humidity (especially at night) combined with warm temperatures.

chemical: Apply fungicides (e.g., sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, myclobutanil, strobilurins) starting when symptoms first appear or preventatively.
Follow label instructions.
organic: Sulfur sprays (use caution in hot weather).

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: The cherry tree appears healthy, with well-formed leaves, no signs of leaf curl, spots, powdery mildew, or significant pest damage.
precautions: Apply dormant sprays (e.g., copper) if leaf curl or bacterial canker are common locally.
Prune for good structure and air circulation.
Monitor for pests like aphids and cherry fruit fly.
risk_factors: Wet spring weather (leaf curl, brown rot).
Nearby sources of disease (e.g., wild cherries).

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: A major fungal disease (Cercospora zeae-maydis) causing long, narrow, rectangular tan/gray lesions restricted by leaf veins. Severe infections cause leaves to blight, reducing yield.
precautions: Plant resistant or tolerant corn hybrids (most effective).
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops (at least one year).
Manage crop residue (tillage can bury fungus).
risk_factors: Infected residue from previous corn crops.

chemical: Apply foliar fungicides (strobilurins, triazoles, mixtures) around tasseling (VT) to early silking (R1) if conditions favor disease and resistant hybrids aren't used.
Scouting is crucial.
organic: Focus heavily on prevention (resistant hybrids, rotation, residue management).

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. Characterized by small, circular to elongated, cinnamon-brown, powdery pustules scattered on both leaf surfaces.
precautions: Plant resistant corn hybrids (most common and effective).
risk_factors: Spores blown in from southern regions.

chemical: Fungicide application usually not economical, especially on resistant hybrids.
If applied to susceptible hybrids during severe outbreaks, timing around tasseling is key.
organic: Prevention via resistant hybrids is the main strategy.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Caused by fungus Exserohilum turcicum. Produces large (1-6 inches), elliptical or cigar-shaped, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves, starting on lower leaves.

chemical: Apply foliar fungicides (strobilurins, triazoles, mixtures) early if conditions favor disease on susceptible hybrids (around tasseling).
Scouting is important.
organic: Focus on resistant hybrids, rotation, and residue management.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: The corn plant appears healthy and vigorous, with green leaves free of significant lesions, discoloration, or pest damage.
precautions: Continue recommended agronomic practices for your region.
Scout regularly for common pests (corn borer, rootworm, aphids) and diseases.
Ensure adequate nutrient supply and soil moisture.
risk_factors: Weather conditions favorable for specific diseases (humidity, temperature).
High pest populations in the area.

Grape___Black_rot.description: Serious fungal disease (Guignardia bidwellii). Leaf spots are circular, reddish-brown with black dots (pycnidia). Fruit rot starts pale, turns black, shrivels into a hard 'mummy'.
precautions: Choose resistant grape varieties if suitable.
Remove and destroy all mummified fruit clusters and infected canes.
risk_factors: Warm, humid, wet weather (bud break through mid-summer).
Infected mummies or cane lesions from previous year.

chemical: Implement a rigorous preventative fungicide spray program (start early, repeat 7-14 days through veraison).
Effective fungicides include mancozeb, captan, myclobutanil, strobilurins (rotate classes).
organic: Copper and sulfur sprays applied preventatively offer some suppression.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Complex, destructive fungal trunk disease involving several fungi. Symptoms include 'tiger stripe' leaf patterns, 'measles' spots on berries, and internal wood decay, often leading to sudden vine collapse (apoplexy).
precautions: Protect pruning wounds (sealant or delayed pruning).
Avoid vine stress (overcropping, poor water management).
risk_factors: Large pruning wounds exposed during wet weather.
Certain varieties may be more susceptible.

chemical: No effective chemical cure currently available for fungi inside the wood.
organic: Focus entirely on prevention (careful pruning, sanitation).

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Fungal disease (Pseudocercospora vitis). Irregular, brown to black spots on leaves, often near margins, merging into larger blighted areas. Small dark fungal structures may be visible. Can cause defoliation.
precautions: Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn/winter.
Ensure good air circulation (pruning, canopy management).

chemical: Fungicides used for Black Rot/Downy Mildew (mancozeb, captan, copper, strobilurins) usually provide control.
Specific sprays usually not needed unless severe.
organic: Copper or sulfur sprays may offer suppression.

Grape___healthy.description: The grape vine appears healthy, with normal leaf color and development, no significant spots or powdery growth, and healthy-looking shoots and fruit clusters.
precautions: Follow recommended spray schedule for common diseases (black rot, downy/powdery mildew) based on local conditions.
Practice proper pruning and canopy management for air circulation.
Monitor for pests (e.g., grape berry moth, Japanese beetle, leafhoppers).
risk_factors: Weather conditions favorable for fungal diseases (humidity, rain).

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Devastating bacterial disease (Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus) spread by Asian Citrus Psyllid. Affects vascular system, causing yellow shoots, blotchy mottled leaves, poor growth, bitter/misshapen fruit, and eventual tree death.
precautions: Strictly control Asian Citrus Psyllid populations (insecticides, biological control).
Remove and destroy infected trees immediately.
risk_factors: Presence of the Asian Citrus Psyllid vector.
Warm climates favorable for psyllid reproduction.

chemical: No cure for HLB.
Management focuses on intensive psyllid control (timed insecticide sprays, rotation of modes of action - consult local ICAR/university recommendations).
organic: Psyllid control is very difficult organically (horticultural oils, kaolin clay, natural enemies).
Enhanced nutritional programs may prolong tree life but do not cure.
Removal of infected trees is key.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial disease (Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni). Affects leaves, fruit, twigs. Causes small, angular, water-soaked leaf spots turning purplish-black, falling out ('shot-hole'), leading to yellowing/defoliation. Fruit lesions are small, circular, sunken spots.
precautions: Plant resistant or tolerant peach varieties (most effective).
Select well-drained sites with good air circulation.
Prune during dry weather, sterilize tools.
risk_factors: Twig cankers or infected buds from previous season.

chemical: Copper bactericides during dormancy can reduce overwintering bacteria.
Growing season sprays (copper - low rates, oxytetracycline) offer limited suppression during wet periods.
Consult local recommendations.
organic: Focus on resistant varieties and cultural practices.

Peach___healthy.description: The peach tree shows no signs of significant disease or pest infestation. Leaves are healthy, growth is normal, and fruit (if present) is developing well.
precautions: Continue good cultural practices: watering, balanced fertilization.
Monitor for pests (aphids, borers, oriental fruit moth) and diseases (leaf curl, brown rot, bacterial spot).
Implement preventative spray programs (e.g., dormant sprays) if needed locally.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Common bacterial disease (Xanthomonas spp.). Causes small, water-soaked leaf spots enlarging to brown/black, often with yellow halos. Centers may fall out. Fruit spots are raised and scab-like.
precautions: Practice crop rotation (3+ years from peppers/tomatoes).

chemical: Preventative copper bactericides (often mixed with mancozeb) can reduce spread but are not curative.
Acibenzolar-S-methyl (ASM) products boost plant defenses.
Consult local extension services.
organic: Focus heavily on prevention (resistant varieties, clean seed, rotation, sanitation).

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: The bell pepper plant appears vigorous and healthy, with green leaves free from spots or distortion. Flowers and fruit are developing normally.
precautions: Monitor regularly for pests (aphids, thrips, mites) and diseases.

Potato___Early_blight.description: Fungal disease (Alternaria solani). Dark brown/black spots ('target spots' with rings) on lower leaves first. Leaves yellow and die. Can cause stem lesions and tuber rot.
risk_factors: Warm temps (75-85°F), high humidity, rain/overhead irrigation.

chemical: Apply protective fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, copper, azoxystrobin) preventatively or at first sign.
Repeat applications as needed.

Potato___Late_blight.description: Devastating oomycete disease (Phytophthora infestans). Large, rapidly spreading, water-soaked lesions on leaves (white fuzz underside in humidity). Stem lesions, tuber rot. Can destroy fields quickly.
risk_factors: Cool temps (60-70°F), high humidity (>90%) or wet weather.

chemical: Preventative fungicide program is essential in prone areas (start before disease).
Use effective fungicides (cymoxanil, mandipropamid, fluopicolide, chlorothalonil, mancozeb - rotate modes).
Curative action is limited.
organic: Focus heavily on resistant varieties and sanitation.

Potato___healthy.description: The potato plant shows normal, vigorous growth with healthy foliage and appropriate tuber development underground.
precautions: Monitor for pests (Colorado potato beetle, aphids, leafhoppers, tuber moth) and diseases.

Raspberry___healthy.description: The raspberry cane appears vigorous with healthy green leaves, growing well without signs of spots, wilting, or pest damage.
precautions: Practice proper pruning (remove old floricanes post-harvest, manage primocanes).
Ensure good air circulation (thinning, weed control).
Monitor for pests (mites, cane borer, SWD) and diseases (cane blight, anthracnose, rusts).

Soybean___healthy.description: The soybean plant shows normal development with healthy green trifoliate leaves and appropriate pod set (if applicable) for its growth stage.
precautions: Select varieties resistant to local diseases.
Monitor for pests (aphids, pod feeders) and diseases.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Common fungal disease on cucurbits. White, powdery spots on leaves, stems. Leaves yellow, dry out, reducing yield/quality.

chemical: Apply fungicides (sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, myclobutanil, strobilurins, tebuconazole) at first sign or preventatively.
Ensure thorough coverage.
Rotate modes of action.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Fungal disease (Diplocarpon earlianum). Numerous small, irregular, purplish spots on leaves, merging to look 'scorched'. Leaves dry and curl.

chemical: Apply fungicides (captan, myclobutanil, strobilurins) starting early spring before bloom.
Repeat as needed.

Strawberry___healthy.description: The strawberry plant shows vigorous growth with healthy green leaves, runners, and developing flowers/fruit.
precautions: Maintain soil fertility and slightly acidic pH.
Monitor for pests (mites, aphids, slugs) and diseases (leaf spots, fruit rots).

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial disease (Xanthomonas spp.). Small, water-soaked leaf spots turning brown/black with yellow halos. Fruit spots raised/scab-like.

chemical: Preventative copper + mancozeb sprays.
Acibenzolar-S-methyl (ASM) products.
Limited effectiveness once established.

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Fungal disease (Alternaria solani). Dark 'target spots' on lower leaves. Defoliation progresses upward. Can cause stem lesions, fruit rot near stem.

chemical: Preventative fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, copper, azoxystrobin).
Repeat applications.

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Devastating oomycete (Phytophthora infestans). Large, rapidly spreading water-soaked lesions (white fuzz underside in humidity). Stem lesions. Greasy brown blotches on fruit.

chemical: Preventative fungicide program essential.
Use effective fungicides, rotate modes (cymoxanil, mandipropamid, fluopicolide, chlorothalonil, mancozeb).

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Fungal disease (Fulvia fulva). Affects greenhouse/high humidity tomatoes. Pale spots upper leaf, fuzzy olive-green mold underside. Leaves yellow/die.

chemical: Fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, copper) preventatively.
Ensure lower leaf coverage.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Fungal disease (Septoria lycopersici). Numerous small spots (~1/8 inch) with dark borders, tan centers, small black dots (pycnidia). Starts lower leaves. Causes yellowing, withering, defoliation.

chemical: Fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, copper) preventatively or at first sign.
Repeat applications.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Pest (Tetranychus urticae). Tiny arachnids feeding on underside of leaves. Causes fine yellow/white speckling (stippling). Leaves yellow/bronze/dry. Fine webbing in heavy infestations.

chemical: Specific miticides (abamectin, bifenthrin, spiromesifen - rotate classes).
Check local registrations.
organic: Insecticidal soap or horticultural oil (thorough coverage underside).

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Fungal disease (Corynespora cassiicola). Small dark spots enlarge with concentric rings ('target spot'). Can affect leaves, stems, fruit (small, sunken dark spots).

chemical: Apply fungicides effective against leaf spots (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, strobilurins) preventatively or at first sign.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Devastating viral disease (TYLCV) transmitted by whiteflies. Severe stunting, upward curling/yellowing of leaf margins, reduced leaf size. Significant yield loss.
precautions: Control whitefly populations (reflective mulch, soaps/oils, insecticides, netting).

chemical: No cure.
Focus on intensive whitefly control (insecticides - rotate modes, soil drenches).
organic: Reflective mulches, sticky traps, soaps/oils for whiteflies.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Viral disease (ToMV). Mottled light/dark green mosaic pattern on leaves, stunting, leaf distortion. Can reduce yield.
risk_factors: Handling plants after using tobacco products.

Tomato___healthy.description: The tomato plant is growing well with healthy green leaves, setting flowers, and developing fruit appropriately.
